enrichment.short_description: Orb is a usage-based billing and revenue automation platform that lets B2B software and AI companies meter product usage and launch hybrid pricing models without custom engineering.
enrichment.seo_title: Orb Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Orb review: usage-based billing and revenue automation for B2B software and AI companies. Features, custom pricing, and top alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Orb (withorb.com) is a usage-based billing and revenue automation platform built for B2B software and AI companies that need to meter product usage and launch complex, hybrid pricing models without custom engineering. The company was founded in 2021 by Alvaro Morales and Kshitij Grover, two former engineering leaders at Asana who grew frustrated with the limitations of existing billing tools and set out to build billing infrastructure flexible enough for modern usage-based and consumption pricing. Orb launched publicly in March 2023 and is headquartered in San Francisco, California. Its core product ingests raw product usage events into what Orb calls a Revenue Graph, then lets finance, product, and engineering teams define pricing logic on top of that data covering subscription, usage-based, and hybrid models, generate accurate invoices (including backdated and backfilled data), enforce spend controls and usage alerts, and run revenue simulations before launching new pricing. Orb also offers Experience Kit for customer-facing billing dashboards and checkout, and supports ASC 606-aligned, audit-ready revenue workflows. Its main differentiators versus legacy billing systems are speed of iterating on pricing through version-controlled, rapid-deploy price changes, native support for high-volume event ingestion needed by AI and infrastructure companies billing by API call or token, and deep integrations with Salesforce, NetSuite, and data warehouses. Pricing is entirely custom and quote-based, structured around two metrics: billings (the total value of invoices issued to end customers through Orb) and events (raw usage records ingested), with Core, Advanced, and Enterprise tiers; Advanced and Enterprise add a platform fee plus premium support and enterprise SLAs. Orb's target customers are growth-stage and enterprise B2B software companies, particularly in AI infrastructure, cloud platforms, and developer tools; publicly named customers include Vercel, Supabase, Redis, and LaunchDarkly. In June 2026 payments company Adyen announced a definitive agreement to acquire Orb for 335 million dollars to unify billing and payments infrastructure for enterprise merchants; the deal closed on July 1, 2026, making Orb an indirect, wholly owned subsidiary of Adyen operated under an incubator model. Before the acquisition Orb had raised roughly 44 million dollars from investors including Greylock and Menlo Ventures. Orb's main competitors in the usage-based billing and monetization space include Metronome, Zuora, Chargebee, and Stripe Billing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Orb is a billing and revenue automation platform purpose-built for B2B software companies that need to charge for usage rather than, or in addition to, flat subscriptions. Instead of hard-coding pricing logic into an application, teams send raw usage events to Orb and define pricing rules on top, letting finance and product teams change pricing without waiting on an engineering sprint.

Founded in 2021 by former Asana engineers Alvaro Morales and Kshitij Grover, Orb launched publicly in 2023 and quickly became known among AI infrastructure, cloud platform, and developer-tools companies that bill customers by API calls, compute, tokens, or other consumption metrics. In 2026 the company was acquired by payments giant Adyen to unify billing and payments infrastructure for enterprise merchants.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Orb's core is real-time event ingestion paired with flexible price modeling, so a company can support tiered, volume, per-unit, or hybrid subscription-plus-usage pricing from a single system. Invoicing supports backdating and backfilling usage data, which matters for companies that need to correct or reconcile metering after the fact.

Beyond billing mechanics, Orb includes spend controls and usage alerts, a customer-facing Experience Kit for dashboards and self-serve checkout, revenue simulation tools to model the financial impact of a pricing change before shipping it, and audit-ready workflows aligned to ASC 606 revenue recognition standards.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Orb does not publish flat-rate pricing. Instead, cost is based on two metrics: billings, the total value of invoices issued to a company's end customers through Orb, and events, the volume of raw usage records ingested into the platform.

Orb offers three named tiers: Core, Advanced, and Enterprise. Core covers the essential metering, invoicing, and pricing engine; Advanced (the most commonly adopted tier) adds a platform fee along with data warehouse sync, Salesforce and NetSuite integrations, customer hierarchy, and premium support; Enterprise adds dedicated support and enterprise-grade SLAs. All tiers require contacting Orb's sales team for a quote.

faqs.question: What is Orb used for?
faqs.answer: Orb is used by B2B software companies to meter product usage and automate billing, invoicing, and revenue recognition for usage-based and hybrid pricing models.

faqs.question: How much does Orb cost?
faqs.answer: Orb's pricing is custom and based on billings (invoice value issued through Orb) and events (usage records ingested), with Core, Advanced, and Enterprise tiers available on request.

faqs.question: Is Orb a payment processor?
faqs.answer: No, Orb is a billing and metering engine that integrates with finance, tax, and payment systems rather than processing payments itself.

faqs.question: Who owns Orb?
faqs.answer: Orb was acquired by Adyen in a deal announced in June 2026 and closed on July 1, 2026, and now operates as a wholly owned Adyen subsidiary.

faqs.question: What companies use Orb?
faqs.answer: Publicly named Orb customers include Vercel, Supabase, Redis, and LaunchDarkly.

faqs.question: Does Orb support hybrid pricing models?
faqs.answer: Yes, Orb supports subscription, usage-based, and hybrid pricing combining both within the same contract.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Orb's Core, Advanced, and Enterprise tiers?
faqs.answer: Core covers essential metering and invoicing; Advanced adds a platform fee plus data warehouse sync, CRM/ERP integrations, and premium support; Enterprise adds dedicated support and enterprise SLAs.

faqs.question: Who are Orb's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Orb competes with Metronome, Zuora, Chargebee, and Stripe Billing in the usage-based billing space.

enrichment.short_description: Orca Security is an agentless cloud-native application protection platform (CNAPP) that scans AWS, Azure, GCP, and Kubernetes environments for vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, and compliance risks without deploying agents.
enrichment.seo_title: Orca Security Review 2026: Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Orca Security review: agentless CNAPP for AWS, Azure, and GCP. SideScanning technology, key features, custom pricing, and top competitors compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Orca Security (orca.security) is an agentless cloud-native application protection platform (CNAPP) that identifies, prioritizes, and helps remediate security risks and compliance issues across AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, and Kubernetes environments. The company was founded in 2018 by Avi Shua and Gil Geron, cybersecurity veterans who previously worked together, with Shua serving as CEO; Orca is headquartered in Tel Aviv, Israel, with a significant US presence including offices in Portland, Oregon. Orca's core differentiator is its patented SideScanning technology, which reads cloud workload data directly from the cloud provider's infrastructure layer rather than requiring an agent to be installed on every virtual machine, container, or serverless function, reducing deployment friction and eliminating coverage gaps caused by unmonitored assets. On top of SideScanning, Orca layers a Unified Data Model that correlates vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, identity and entitlement risk (CIEM), malware, and sensitive data exposure into prioritized attack-path narratives rather than raw alert lists, and it has since added Orca Sensor for runtime observability, an AI reasoning engine for automated triage, and dedicated modules for AI security posture and code security scanning earlier in the development lifecycle. Orca has grown quickly, raising a total of about 632 million dollars in venture funding, including a 550 million dollar extended Series C at a 1.8 billion dollar valuation led by Temasek with participation from CapitalG, Redpoint Ventures, GGV, and ICONIQ Capital, reaching unicorn status within roughly two years of launch. The company reports more than 500 employees as of 2026. Orca targets mid-market and enterprise security teams, cloud security, DevSecOps, and compliance groups at organizations running multi-cloud or hybrid-cloud infrastructure who need broad, fast-to-deploy visibility without managing agent fleets; named customers include Autodesk, Swiggy, Sisense, and C6 Bank. Pricing is not published and is sold through a custom, sales-led enterprise contract model based on workload counts and modules purchased. Orca competes most directly with Wiz, Palo Alto Networks Prisma Cloud, Lacework, and Aqua Security in the fast-consolidating CNAPP category, where agentless-versus-agent-based coverage and unified risk prioritization are the primary points of differentiation among vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Orca Security is a cloud security platform that gives security teams full visibility into risk across AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, and Kubernetes without installing agents on every workload. It was founded in 2018 by Avi Shua and Gil Geron and is headquartered in Tel Aviv, Israel, with additional operations in the United States.

The company built its reputation on SideScanning, a patented agentless scanning approach that reads workload data directly from the cloud provider's control plane. This lets security teams onboard an entire cloud estate in minutes rather than deploying agents to thousands of individual resources, a common bottleneck with older cloud workload protection tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Orca combines cloud security posture management (CSPM), cloud workload protection (CWPP), cloud infrastructure entitlement management (CIEM), vulnerability management, and compliance monitoring into a single platform built on a Unified Data Model, so findings across categories are correlated into prioritized attack paths instead of siloed alert feeds.

More recent additions include Orca Sensor for runtime detection and response, an AI reasoning engine that automates parts of triage and remediation, AI security posture management for organizations deploying AI models and agents in the cloud, and code security scanning that catches risks before they reach production. The platform integrates with more than 20 tools including Slack, Jira, ServiceNow, Splunk, and GitHub.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Orca Security does not publish pricing on its website. Cost is determined through a custom, sales-led process typically based on the number of cloud workloads, accounts, and modules (such as CIEM, AI security, or code security) an organization needs.
Prospective customers request a personalized demo, after which Orca's sales team provides a quote scoped to the size and complexity of the customer's cloud environment.

features.title: Compliance monitoring
features.description: Tracks adherence to frameworks such as SOC 2, PCI-DSS, and HIPAA across the cloud estate.

Competes in a crowded, fast-consolidating CNAPP market
Best value requires adopting multiple modules rather than a single point solution

faqs.answer: Orca is primarily agentless, using SideScanning to read workload data from the cloud provider's infrastructure layer, though it also offers an optional Orca Sensor for runtime protection.

enrichment.short_description: Order.co (formerly Negotiatus) is a procurement and spend management platform that centralizes purchasing, accounts payable automation, and vendor payments for finance and operations teams.
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Order.co Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Order.co review: procurement and AP automation platform for centralized purchasing and spend management. Features, custom pricing, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Order.co (order.co) is a procurement and spend management platform that centralizes business purchasing, accounts payable automation, and vendor payments for finance and operations teams. The company was founded in 2016 in New York City as Negotiatus by Zachary Garippa, Brendan Eapen, and Tom Jaklitsch, originally focused on helping office managers consolidate supply purchasing across multiple vendors onto a single invoice and payment. After growing into a broader procure-to-pay platform, the company rebranded from Negotiatus to Order.co in January 2022 alongside a 30 million dollar Series B round led by Stage 2 Capital that included a direct investment from MIT's endowment. Order.co is headquartered in New York and remains privately held. The platform's core functionality spans purchase order creation and vendor fulfillment, automated invoice capture and matching, expense coding, approval workflows for policy enforcement, and virtual cards for payment control and visibility, all syncing back into a company's existing ERP or accounting system for accurate reporting and forecasting. A more recent differentiator is Order.co AI, which learns from purchase history to recommend better prices and alternative products, with the company citing average savings of around five percent on product costs for customers who use it. Order.co is particularly suited to multi-location and multi-entity businesses, including retail chains, restaurant groups, coworking operators, and franchises, that need centralized visibility and control over decentralized purchasing, as well as finance teams looking to automate accounts payable without replacing their accounting system. Pricing is not published; prospective customers get a personalized demo and custom quote based on transaction volume and vendor complexity, though a free trial is available. Order.co competes with procurement and spend management platforms such as Procurify, Coupa, Airbase, and Ramp, differentiating itself through its purchasing-first, vendor-consolidation approach rather than starting from corporate card or travel and expense spend management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Order.co is a procurement and spend management platform designed to give finance and operations teams a single place to purchase from any vendor, automate accounts payable, and control company spend. It began life in 2016 as Negotiatus, a New York startup focused on consolidating office supply purchasing, before rebranding to Order.co in 2022 as its scope expanded into full procure-to-pay workflows.

The platform is aimed at organizations that buy from many different vendors and want to standardize purchasing, approvals, and payments without forcing every vendor onto a single marketplace. It is especially popular with multi-location businesses such as retail chains, restaurant groups, and coworking operators.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Order.co centralizes purchase order creation, vendor fulfillment tracking, and invoice matching, while automating expense coding as soon as an order is placed. Built-in approval workflows let finance teams enforce spend policy before a purchase is made rather than reconciling after the fact.

The platform also offers virtual cards for payment control and visibility, syncs spend data with accounting and ERP systems for forecasting and reporting, and includes Order.co AI, which surfaces better prices and alternative products by learning from a company's purchase history.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Order.co does not publish pricing tiers on its website. Businesses request a demo and receive a custom quote based on their purchasing volume, vendor mix, and the number of locations or entities they need to support.
A free trial is available for businesses that want to evaluate the platform before committing to a paid plan.

enrichment.short_description: Ordway is a subscription billing and revenue automation platform that helps SaaS, usage-based, and fintech companies manage quote-to-cash, invoicing, and revenue recognition.
enrichment.seo_title: Ordway Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ordway review: subscription billing and revenue recognition platform for SaaS companies. Quote-to-cash features, custom pricing, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Ordway (ordwaylabs.com) is a subscription billing and revenue automation platform that helps SaaS, usage-based, and fintech companies manage the full quote-to-cash cycle, from pricing and invoicing through revenue recognition and reporting. The company was founded in 2018 by Sameer Gulati, who serves as CEO and was previously one of Zuora's first product managers before becoming Chief Product Officer at Spree Commerce, which was acquired by First Data; that billing-industry background shaped Ordway's focus on the operational pain points, manual invoicing, spreadsheet-based revenue recognition, and disconnected CRM and finance systems, that mid-market SaaS finance teams face as they scale. Ordway is headquartered in Washington, D.C., and has raised institutional funding from investors including Rise of the Rest Seed Fund, Lerer Hippeau, Clocktower Technology Ventures, and CRV. The platform's core capabilities include flexible subscription and usage-based (metered) billing, automated invoicing and dunning, ASC 606 and IFRS 15 revenue recognition automation, CPQ-style quoting, multi-entity and multi-currency support, and reporting on SaaS metrics such as MRR and churn, all exposed through an API-first architecture designed to plug into existing CRM, ERP, and payment stacks such as Salesforce, NetSuite, and Stripe. Ordway targets mid-market SaaS, cloud, and fintech companies with complex or evolving pricing models, particularly those blending flat subscriptions with usage-based or transaction-based pricing, who have outgrown basic invoicing tools but are not yet ready for the cost and implementation overhead of enterprise platforms like Zuora. Pricing is not publicly listed and is quote-based, tailored to transaction volume, billing complexity, and company size. Ordway's main competitors in the subscription billing and revenue automation space include Zuora, Chargebee, Recurly, and BillingPlatform, with Ordway generally positioning itself as a more configurable, faster-to-implement alternative to legacy enterprise billing suites for growth-stage companies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ordway is a billing and revenue automation platform built for SaaS, cloud, and fintech companies managing subscription, usage-based, or hybrid pricing. It was founded in 2018 by Sameer Gulati, a former Zuora product manager, and is headquartered in Washington, D.C.

The platform covers the full quote-to-cash lifecycle, from quoting and contract creation through invoicing, payment collection, and revenue recognition, aiming to replace the spreadsheets and manual processes many growing SaaS finance teams rely on before adopting dedicated billing software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core functionality includes flexible subscription and usage-based billing, automated invoicing and dunning for failed payments, and revenue recognition automation aligned to ASC 606 and IFRS 15 standards. Ordway also supports CPQ-style quoting, multi-entity and multi-currency billing, and reporting on core SaaS metrics like MRR, churn, and revenue waterfall.

Because the platform is API-first, it is designed to integrate with existing CRM, ERP, and payment systems such as Salesforce, NetSuite, and Stripe rather than requiring a company to replace its broader finance stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Ordway does not publish pricing on its website. Cost is determined through a custom sales process based on transaction volume, billing complexity, and the number of entities or currencies a company needs to support.
Prospective customers contact Ordway's sales team directly to scope requirements and receive a quote.

enrichment.seo_title: Orgvue Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Orgvue review: organizational design and workforce planning software with scenario modeling and skills analysis. Features, pricing, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Orgvue (orgvue.com) is a cloud-based organizational design and workforce planning platform that lets HR, strategy, and business leaders import and harmonize workforce data, visualize org structures, and model scenarios for restructuring, mergers and acquisitions integration, and headcount planning. The company was founded in 2005 by Rupert Morrison and is headquartered in London, United Kingdom, having grown from an organizational consulting background into a dedicated software platform; it has raised a total of about 56.4 million dollars in funding, including investment from Morgan Stanley, and reports roughly 260 employees as of 2026. Orgvue's core differentiator versus generic business intelligence or spreadsheet-based org planning is that it is purpose-built around organizational and workforce data models: it connects to and cleans data from multiple HR and finance systems to build a trusted baseline, then lets users build interactive org charts and dashboards, run what-if scenario modeling to compare the cost and structural impact of alternative organizational designs in real time, and map skills and competency taxonomies to analyze workforce capability gaps against future demand. These capabilities make it especially useful for large-scale reorganizations, cost-reduction initiatives, and strategic workforce planning tied to skills and future-of-work analysis, rather than day-to-day HRIS record-keeping. Orgvue's target customers are HR, people analytics, and organizational design teams at large and mid-size enterprises undertaking complex restructuring, M&A integration, or long-range workforce planning, typically working alongside or on top of a core HCM system such as Workday or SAP SuccessFactors rather than replacing it. Pricing is not publicly disclosed and is sold through an enterprise, quote-based sales process. Orgvue competes with organizational design and workforce planning tools such as Visier and ChartHop, as well as the workforce planning modules within Workday and SAP SuccessFactors, differentiating itself through its dedicated focus on org modeling and skills-based scenario planning rather than being a module bolted onto a broader HCM suite.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Orgvue is a workforce planning and organizational design platform used by large enterprises to model and visualize how their organization is structured and how it might need to change. Founded in 2005 by Rupert Morrison and headquartered in London, the company grew out of organizational design consulting work into a dedicated software product.

Rather than replacing a core HR information system, Orgvue sits alongside systems like Workday or SAP SuccessFactors, pulling in workforce data to support the kind of scenario planning, restructuring, and skills analysis that transactional HR systems are not built to do.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Orgvue's foundation is data integration and harmonization: connecting and cleaning workforce data from multiple source systems into a single trusted baseline that can be segmented by organization, position, and people dimensions. On top of that data, users build interactive org charts and dashboards for a clear, real-time view of the organization.

The platform's scenario modeling lets teams simulate alternative organizational designs, comparing cost and structural impact side by side to build consensus before committing to a restructuring plan. Its skills and competency analysis maps the skills required for each role, visualizes skills distribution across the workforce, and highlights gaps against future workforce demand.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Orgvue does not publish pricing publicly. As an enterprise platform aimed at large organizations undertaking complex workforce planning projects, it is sold through a custom, quote-based sales process.
Pricing typically depends on the number of users, the size of the workforce being modeled, and which modules, such as scenario modeling or skills analysis, a customer needs.

features.title: Workforce demand and supply forecasting
features.description: Models demand, supply, and gap assumptions to plan future headcount needs.

features.title: Collaborative planning
features.description: Supports role-based collaboration among HR and business stakeholders on org design projects.

features.title: HR system integrations
features.description: Integrates with core HCM platforms such as Workday and SAP SuccessFactors.

features.title: Reporting and dashboards
features.description: Provides reporting views for tracking org design and workforce planning outcomes.

Strong scenario modeling for restructuring and M&A workforce planning
Visual, intuitive org charting even with large, complex datasets
Dedicated skills-based workforce planning capability
Used by large enterprises for complex reorganization projects
Backed by institutional investment including Morgan Stanley, signaling enterprise credibility
cons: Pricing is not public and requires an enterprise sales process
Steep learning curve when building custom data models
Best suited to larger organizations with dedicated HR or org design analysts
Effectiveness depends on the quality of underlying HR data fed into the platform
Competes with workforce planning modules bundled into broader HCM suites
Not designed as a system of record, so it requires integration with a core HRIS

faqs.question: What is Orgvue used for?
faqs.answer: Orgvue is used for organizational design and workforce planning, including visualizing org structure, modeling restructuring scenarios, and analyzing workforce skills.

faqs.question: Who founded Orgvue?
faqs.answer: Orgvue was founded in 2005 by Rupert Morrison and is headquartered in London, United Kingdom.

faqs.question: How much does Orgvue cost?
faqs.answer: Orgvue does not publish pricing; it is sold through a custom, quote-based enterprise sales process.

faqs.question: Does Orgvue replace a core HR system?
faqs.answer: No, Orgvue is typically used alongside a core HCM system such as Workday or SAP SuccessFactors rather than replacing it.

faqs.question: What kind of companies use Orgvue?
faqs.answer: Orgvue is used by large and mid-size enterprises undertaking complex restructuring, M&A integration, or strategic workforce planning.

faqs.question: Does Orgvue support skills-based workforce planning?
faqs.answer: Yes, Orgvue includes skills and competency analysis tools that map skills by role and identify workforce capability gaps.

faqs.question: Can Orgvue model different organizational scenarios?
faqs.answer: Yes, Orgvue's scenario modeling lets users compare the cost and structural impact of alternative organizational designs in real time.

faqs.question: Who are Orgvue's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Orgvue competes with Visier, ChartHop, and the workforce planning modules within Workday and SAP SuccessFactors.

enrichment.short_description: Originality.ai is an AI content detection, plagiarism checking, and fact-checking platform used by publishers, content marketers, and agencies to verify the authenticity of written content.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Originality.ai review: AI content detector, plagiarism checker, and fact checker for publishers and marketers. Pricing, features, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Originality.ai (originality.ai) is an AI content detection, plagiarism checking, and fact-checking platform aimed at publishers, content marketers, SEO agencies, and educators who need to verify whether text was written by a human or generated by tools like ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Grok, and whether it has been plagiarized. The company was founded in 2022 by Jon Gillham and Conor Watt and is headquartered in Collingwood, Ontario, Canada, operating as a lean team of roughly 20 employees. Originality.ai's core differentiator is combining several verification layers in one tool: an AI detector the company positions as highly accurate against major AI models, a plagiarism checker covering global, paraphrased, and patchwork plagiarism, and a fact checker that cross-references written claims against external sources to flag inaccuracies before publication, alongside readability, grammar, and an SEO content optimizer, plus a Chrome extension and website or domain scanning for ongoing content audits. It grew out of the content marketing industry's need, after the launch of ChatGPT, for a reliable way to police AI-generated content at scale for SEO and editorial quality purposes. Pricing is credit-based and subscription-driven rather than a flat monthly fee: a Pro plan starts at roughly 14.95 dollars per month, or about 12.95 dollars per month billed annually, for 2,000 monthly credits, an Enterprise plan runs about 179 dollars per month, or about 136.58 dollars per month billed annually, for 15,000 credits plus API access and priority support, and a pay-as-you-go option offers 3,000 credits for 30 dollars, with one credit covering roughly 100 words of AI-only scanning and two credits per 100 words when combining AI and plagiarism scans. Target users include content agencies, publishers, SEO teams, and academic institutions needing to verify content authenticity at scale. Its primary competitors are Copyleaks, Turnitin, GPTZero, and Winston AI, with Originality.ai generally positioned as a preferred detector among SEO and content-marketing professionals specifically, versus Turnitin's stronger footprint in academic settings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Originality.ai is a content verification platform that detects AI-generated text, checks for plagiarism, and fact-checks written claims. It was founded in 2022 by Jon Gillham and Conor Watt and is headquartered in Collingwood, Ontario, Canada, built specifically for the content marketing and publishing industry's needs following the rise of generative AI writing tools.

The platform is used primarily by SEO agencies, content marketers, publishers, and educators who need a reliable way to confirm whether text was written by a human, generated by an AI model, or copied from another source before it is published or graded.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The AI detector checks text against outputs from major AI models including ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Grok, while the plagiarism checker identifies global, paraphrased, and patchwork plagiarism against web content. A fact checker cross-references claims in the text against reputable sources to flag potential inaccuracies before publication.

Additional tools include a readability and grammar checker, an SEO content optimizer, a Chrome extension for in-browser scanning, website and domain scanning for ongoing content audits, and team management features that let agencies and publishers share credit pools across multiple seats.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Originality.ai uses a credit-based subscription model where one credit covers roughly 100 words of scanning. The Pro plan starts at 14.95 dollars per month billed monthly, or 12.95 dollars per month billed annually, and includes 2,000 monthly credits.

The Enterprise plan costs 179 dollars per month billed monthly, or 136.58 dollars per month billed annually, for 15,000 monthly credits plus API access and a dedicated customer success manager. A pay-as-you-go option is also available for 30 dollars for 3,000 credits, with credits expiring two years after purchase.

features.title: Website and domain scanning
features.description: Scans entire websites or domains to audit content for AI use and plagiarism over time.

features.title: Team management
features.description: Lets agencies and publishers manage multiple seats and shared credit pools.

cons: Credit-based pricing can become expensive at high content volumes
No unlimited free tier for ongoing use

Smaller company size means more limited enterprise support infrastructure
Lowest monthly price requires committing to annual billing
Combined AI and plagiarism scans consume credits faster than single-purpose checks

faqs.question: What does Originality.ai do?
faqs.answer: Originality.ai detects AI-generated text, checks for plagiarism, and fact-checks written content for publishers, marketers, and educators.

faqs.question: How much does Originality.ai cost?
faqs.answer: Originality.ai's Pro plan starts at 14.95 dollars per month (12.95 dollars per month billed annually), with an Enterprise plan and pay-as-you-go credits also available.

faqs.question: How accurate is Originality.ai's AI detector?
faqs.answer: Originality.ai claims high accuracy detecting text from major AI models such as ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Grok, though no AI detector is guaranteed to be error-free.

faqs.question: Does Originality.ai check for plagiarism?
faqs.answer: Yes, Originality.ai includes a plagiarism checker that detects global, paraphrased, and patchwork plagiarism.

faqs.answer: Originality.ai was founded in 2022 by Jon Gillham and Conor Watt.

faqs.question: Does Originality.ai have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, API access is included on the Enterprise plan.

faqs.question: What is a credit in Originality.ai?
faqs.answer: One credit covers approximately 100 words of AI-only scanning, while combined AI and plagiarism scans use two credits per 100 words.

faqs.question: Who are Originality.ai's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Originality.ai competes with Copyleaks, Turnitin, GPTZero, and Winston AI.

enrichment.short_description: OroCRM is an open-source customer relationship management platform from Oro Inc, built for B2B and ecommerce companies that want deep customization and self-hosted deployment options.
enrichment.seo_title: OroCRM Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OroCRM review: open-source CRM from Oro Inc with ecommerce-native architecture. Community and Enterprise Edition pricing, features, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OroCRM is an open-source customer relationship management platform developed by Oro Inc, sold alongside the company's flagship OroCommerce B2B ecommerce platform and built on the shared OroPlatform framework. Oro Inc was founded in 2012 in Los Angeles, California, by Yoav Kutner, Jary Carter, and Dima Soroka, who previously built and led Magento, with Kutner as co-founder and former CTO, Carter as former VP of Sales and Channel, and Soroka as former lead architect; OroCRM itself launched in 2014 as the team's first product before OroCommerce followed in 2017. Oro remains headquartered in Los Angeles and has roughly 100 to 120 employees. OroCRM's key differentiator is that its Community Edition is completely free and open source, with full source code available on GitHub, giving companies the option to self-host and fully customize the CRM rather than depend on a proprietary vendor; a paid Enterprise Edition adds role- and group-based access control with ACLs, localization, sales and marketing dashboards, a flexible workflow engine, case management, advanced customer segmentation, PostgreSQL support for large datasets, and vendor support, and can be deployed on-premise or in Oro's cloud. Because it is built on the same OroPlatform as OroCommerce, OroCRM has particularly deep native integration for B2B and B2C ecommerce companies that want unified customer, order, and marketing data across storefront and CRM. Target users are mid-size and enterprise B2B and ecommerce companies, systems integrators, and organizations with technical teams that want a highly customizable, self-hostable CRM rather than a rigid SaaS-only product, as well as companies with data residency or compliance requirements that favor self-hosting. Pricing for Community Edition is free; Enterprise Edition is quote-based, with third-party estimates citing roughly 116 dollars per user per month as a starting reference point depending on deployment and scale. OroCRM's main competitors include Salesforce, HubSpot, and SugarCRM, as well as other open-source alternatives such as SuiteCRM, with OroCRM's ecommerce-native architecture and open-source flexibility being its main points of differentiation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OroCRM is an open-source CRM platform built by Oro Inc, a company founded in 2012 in Los Angeles by former Magento leaders Yoav Kutner, Jary Carter, and Dima Soroka. OroCRM launched in 2014 as Oro's first product, followed in 2017 by the B2B ecommerce platform OroCommerce, both built on the shared OroPlatform framework.

Because it shares its foundation with OroCommerce, OroCRM is particularly well suited to B2B and ecommerce businesses that want customer, order, and marketing data unified across their storefront and CRM, while its open-source Community Edition gives technical teams full control over customization and hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OroCRM provides a 360-degree customer view combining sales, marketing, and service data, along with a flexible workflow engine that lets companies model custom business processes rather than being locked into rigid, pre-built pipelines. Marketing segmentation and campaign tools support targeted outreach based on customer behavior and order history.

The Enterprise Edition adds role- and group-based access control (ACL), localization for multiple countries and regions, sales and marketing dashboards, case management, and PostgreSQL support for handling large datasets, and can be deployed on-premise or in Oro's own cloud.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OroCRM's Community Edition is free and open source, available for download and self-hosting by any organization, including small businesses that want a no-cost, fully customizable CRM.

The Enterprise Edition is aimed at medium and large organizations and is priced on a custom, quote-based basis; third-party estimates place typical starting costs around 116 dollars per user per month, though actual pricing depends on deployment model, user count, and required modules.

features.description: Supports self-hosted on-premise deployment or Oro's managed cloud.

features.description: Enterprise Edition includes PostgreSQL support for handling high-volume customer data.

Deep customization through the flexible OroPlatform architecture
Strong native fit for B2B and ecommerce businesses via OroCommerce integration
Self-hosting option appeals to companies with data residency or compliance needs
Built by a team with prior Magento founding experience
Active GitHub-based development community
cons: Requires technical resources to self-host and maintain the Community Edition
Smaller ecosystem and app marketplace than Salesforce or HubSpot
Enterprise Edition pricing is not fully transparent and is quote-based
Steeper learning curve than simpler out-of-the-box CRMs
Smaller company size means a smaller third-party support network
Best value requires technical teams comfortable with platform customization

faqs.question: Is OroCRM free?
faqs.answer: The Community Edition of OroCRM is free and open source; the Enterprise Edition is paid and quote-based.

faqs.question: Who makes OroCRM?
faqs.answer: OroCRM is made by Oro Inc, founded in 2012 in Los Angeles by former Magento leaders Yoav Kutner, Jary Carter, and Dima Soroka.

faqs.question: Can OroCRM be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, OroCRM can be deployed on-premise for self-hosting or in Oro's own cloud.

faqs.question: How does OroCRM relate to OroCommerce?
faqs.answer: OroCRM and OroCommerce are both built on the shared OroPlatform framework, allowing unified customer, order, and marketing data across CRM and ecommerce.

faqs.question: How much does OroCRM Enterprise Edition cost?
faqs.answer: OroCRM Enterprise Edition is quote-based, with third-party estimates citing roughly 116 dollars per user per month as a starting reference point.

faqs.question: Is OroCRM open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Community Edition source code is publicly available on GitHub under an open-source license.

faqs.question: Who is OroCRM best suited for?
faqs.answer: OroCRM is best suited for mid-size and enterprise B2B and ecommerce companies with technical teams that want deep CRM customization.

faqs.question: Who are OroCRM's main competitors?
faqs.answer: OroCRM competes with Salesforce, HubSpot, SugarCRM, and open-source alternative SuiteCRM.

enrichment.short_description: Ortto (formerly Autopilot) is a marketing automation and customer data platform that unifies customer data, multi-channel messaging, and analytics; it was acquired by Canva in April 2026.
enrichment.seo_title: Ortto Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ortto review: customer data and marketing automation platform, now part of Canva. Pricing plans, key features, and top alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Ortto (ortto.com), formerly known as Autopilot, is a marketing automation and customer data platform that unifies customer data with multi-channel messaging, including email, SMS, push notifications, in-app messages, forms, and surveys, inside a single visual customer journey builder. The company was founded in 2012 in Bondi Beach, Australia, by brothers Michael, Chris, and Peter Sharkey, initially as Bislr before adopting the Autopilot name and product in 2015; the founders relocated the company back to Australia in 2018 to rebuild the platform, and in March 2022 it was rebranded from Autopilot to Ortto to reflect an expanded focus on unifying customer data, messaging, and analytics rather than automation alone. Ortto is headquartered in Sydney, Australia, with a US presence in San Francisco, and serves more than 11,000 customers across 190 countries, including Atlassian, Microsoft, and Patreon. Its core differentiator is combining customer data platform functionality, ingesting behavioral, transactional, and demographic data, directly with marketing automation and reporting in one product, reducing the need to stitch together separate CDP, email service provider, and analytics tools. Pricing is subscription-based and scales with contact volume: a Starter plan begins around 199 dollars per month for 5,000 contacts, Professional plans start around 509 dollars per month with annual billing for 10,000 contacts, a Business plan runs about 849 dollars per month annually with more advanced analytics, and Enterprise plans are custom-quoted, with a 14-day free trial available. Ortto's target users are marketing teams at SaaS, ecommerce, and high-tech companies that want behavior-triggered, multi-channel campaigns without hiring a dedicated marketing operations engineer. In a major strategic shift, Ortto was acquired by design platform Canva in April 2026 alongside AI workspace startup Simtheory, as Canva pushes beyond design tools into marketing automation and agentic AI infrastructure; financial terms were not disclosed. Ortto's principal competitors include HubSpot, ActiveCampaign, and Klaviyo, with Ortto typically differentiating on its unified customer-data-plus-automation architecture and multi-channel, not just email, messaging.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ortto is a marketing automation and customer data platform that combines customer data, multi-channel messaging, and analytics in one product. It was founded in 2012 by Australian brothers Michael, Chris, and Peter Sharkey, originally launching as Autopilot in 2015 before rebranding to Ortto in March 2022 to reflect a broader focus beyond automation alone.

Headquartered in Sydney, Australia, with additional operations in San Francisco, Ortto serves more than 11,000 customers globally, including Atlassian, Microsoft, and Patreon. In April 2026, Ortto was acquired by design platform Canva alongside AI workspace startup Simtheory as part of Canva's expansion into marketing automation and agentic AI.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Ortto ingests behavioral, transactional, and demographic customer data to build a unified customer profile, then lets marketing teams design customer journeys using a visual, drag-and-drop journey builder. Messaging spans email, SMS, push notifications, in-app messages, forms, and surveys rather than being limited to a single channel.

The platform also includes marketing analytics and reporting dashboards, website and product activity tracking, and AI-assisted content and audience segmentation, along with integrations for ecommerce and SaaS tools to trigger campaigns based on real customer behavior.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Ortto's pricing scales with contact volume. The Starter plan begins around 199 dollars per month for up to 5,000 contacts, while Professional plans start around 509 dollars per month on annual billing for 10,000 contacts, and Business plans run about 849 dollars per month annually with more advanced analytics.

Enterprise plans are custom-quoted based on scale and services required. Extra contacts and email volume are available as add-ons, and a 14-day free trial with up to 10,000 contacts is available for new customers.
